title: 第 24 節
en: Grammatical gender, therefore, though it helps very powerfully in the later process of poetical mythology, is not the real motive power. That motive power is inherent in the very nature of language and thought. Man has vocal signs for his own acts, he discovers similar acts in the outward world, and he grasps, he lays hold, he comprehends the various objects of his outward world by the same vocal signs. He never dreams at first, because the river is called a defender, that therefore the river has legs, and arms, and weapons of defence ; or that the moon, because he divides and measures the sky, is a carpenter. Much of this misunderstanding will arise at a later time. At present, we move as yet in much lower strata of thought.
Auxiliary verbs.

We imagine that language is impossible without sentences, and that sentences are impossible without OBJECTS TANGIBLE, SEMI-TANGIBLE, INTANGIBLE. 191 the copula. This view is both right and wrong. If we mean by sentence what it means, namely an utterance that conveys a sense, then it is right : if we mean that it is an utterance consisting of several words, a subject, and a predicate, and a copula, then it is wrong. The mere imperative is a sentence ; every form of the verb may be a sentence. What we now call a noun was originally a kind of sen tence, consisting of the root and some so-called suffix, which pointed to something of which that root was predicated. So again, when there is a subject and a predicate, we may say that a copula is understood, but the truth is that at first it was not expressed, it was not required to be expressed ; nay in primitive languages it was simply impossible to express it. To be able to say vir est bonus, instead of vir bonus, is one of the latest achievements of human speech.

We saw that the early Aryans found it difficult to speak, that is to think, of anything except as active. They had the same difficulties to overcome, when trying to say that a thing simply is or was. They could only express that idea at first, by saying that a thing did something which they did them selves. Now the most general act of all human beings was the act of breathing, and thus, where we say that things are, they said that things breathe.
AS, to breathe.

The root as, which still lives in our he is, is a very old root : it existed in its abstract sense previous to the Aryan separation. Nevertheless we know that as, before it could mean to be, meant to breathe.
The simplest derivation of as, to breathe, was as-u, in Sanskrit, breath ; and from it probably asu-ra, 192 LECTURE IV.
those who breathe, who live, who are, and at last, the oldest name for the living gods, the Vedic A sura1.
BHU, to grow.

When this root as, to breathe, was felt to be in convenient, as applied, for instance, to trees and other things which clearly do not breathe, a second root was takin, }>liu, meaning originally to grow, the Greek (pv-w, which still lives in our own to be. It was applicable, not to the animal world only, but also to the vegetable world, to everything growing, and the earth itself was called Bhus, the growing one.
VAS, to dwell.

Lastly, when a still wider concept was wanted, the root vas was taken, meaning originally to abide, to dwell. We find it in Sanskrit vas-tu, a house, the Greek aa-rv, town, and it still lingers on in the English / was. This could be used of all things which fall neither under the concept of breathing, nor under that of growing. It was the first approach to an expression of impersonal or dead being. There is, in fact, a certain analogy between the formation of masculine, feminine, and neuter nouns and the introduction of these three auxiliary verbs.

1 This Sanskrit asu is the Zend ahu, which in the Avesta has the meanings of conscience and world (see Darmesteter, 'Ormazd et Ahriman/ p. 47). If ahu in Zend is used also in the sense of lord, it does not follow that therefore ahura in Ahura mazda, meant lord, and was formed by a secondary suffix ra. Zend may have assigned to ahu two meanings, breath and lord, as it did in the case of ratu, order and orderer. But to assign to Sanskrit asura the meaning of lord, because Ahu in Zend is used in that sense, seems inadmissible.
OBJECTS TANGIBLE, SEMI-TANGIBLE, INTANGIBLE. 193 Primitive expression.

Let us apply these observations to the way in which it was possible for the early Aryan speakers to say anything about the sun, the moon, the sky, the earth, the mountains and the rivers. When we should say, the moon exists, the sun is there, or it blows, it rains, they could only think and say, the sun breathes (suryo asti), the moon grows (ma bhavati), the earth dwells (bhur vasati), the wind or the blower blows (vayur vati), the rain rains (indra unatti, or vri'sha varshati, or somaA sunoti).

We are speaking here of the earliest attempts at comprehending and expressing the play of nature, which was acted before the eyes of man. We are using Sanskrit only as an illustration of linguistic processes long anterior to Sanskrit. How the com prehension determined the expression, and how the various expressions, in becoming traditional, reacted on the comprehension, how that action and reaction produced by necessity ancient mythology, all these are problems which belong to a later phase of thought, and must not be allowed to detain us at present. One point only there is which cannot be urged too strongly. Because the early Aryans had to call the sun by names expressive of various kinds of activity, because he was called iUuminator or warmer, maker or nourisher, because they called the moon the measurer, the dawn the awakener, the thunder the roarer, the rain the rainer, the fire the quick runner, do not let us suppose that they believed these objects to be human beings, with arms and legs. Even when they still said 'the sun is breathing/ they never meant that the sun was a man or at least an animal, having lungs and a mouth to breathe with. Our troglodyte 194 LECTURE IV.

ancestors were neither idiots nor poets. In saying ' the sun or the nourisher is breathing,' they meant no more than that the sun was active, was up and doing, was moving about like ourselves. The old Aryans did not yet see in the moon two eyes, a nose, and a mouth, nor did they represent to themselves the winds that blew, as so many fat-cheeked urchins, puffing streams of wind from the four corners of the sky. All that will come by and bye, but not in these early days of human thought.
Likeness, originally conceived as negation.

During the stage in which we are now moving, I believe that our Aryan ancestors, so far from anima ting, personifying, or humanizing the objects, which we described as semi-tangible or intangible, were far more struck by the difference between them and themselves than by any imaginary similarities.

And here let me remind you of a curious confir mation of this theory preserved to us in the Veda. What we call comparison is still, in many of the Vedic hymns, negation. Instead of saying as we do, ' firm like a rock/ the poets of the Veda say, ' firm, not a rock1 ;' that is, they lay stress on the dissimilarity, in order to make the similarity to be felt. They offer a hymn of praise to the god, not sweet food 2, that is, as if it were sweet food. The river is said to come near roaring, not a bull, i. e. like a bull ; and the 1 Rig- Veda, I, 52, 2, sa/t parvata/i na akyntah; I, 64, 7, giraya/i na svatavasaA. The na is put after the word which serves as a comparison, so that the original conception was, 'he, a rock, no;' i. e. he not altogether, but only to a certain point, a rock.
2 Rig- Veda, I, 61, i.
OBJECTS TANGIBLE, SEMT-TANGTBLE, INTANGIBLE. 195 Marats or storm -gods are said1 to hold their wor shippers in their arms, ' a father, not the son,' viz. like as a father carries his son in his arms.

Thus the sun and the moon were spoken of, no doubt, as moving about, but not as animals ; the rivers were roaring and fighting, but they were not men; the mountains were not to be thrown down, but they were not warriors ; the fire was eating up the forest, yet it was not a lion.

In translating such passages from the Veda, we always render na, not, by like ; but it is important to observe that the poets themselves were originally struck by the dissimilarity quite as much, if not more than by the similarity.
Standing epithets.

In speaking of these various objects of nature, which from the earliest times excited their attention, the poets would naturally use certain epithets more frequently than others. These objects of nature were different from each other, but they likewise shared a certain number of qualities in common ; they there fore could be called by certain common epithets, and afterwards fall into a class, under each epithet, and thus constitute a new concept. All this was pos sible : — let us see what really happened.

We turn to the Veda, and we find that the hymns which have been preserved to us, are all addressed, according to the views of the old Indian theologians, to certain devatds*. This word devatd corresponds 1 Kig-Veda, I, 38, i.
2 Anukramanika : Yasya vakyam sa rishih, ya teno&yate, sa devata. Tena vakyena pratipadyam yad vastu, sa devata.

exactly to our word deity, but in the hymns themselves devatd never occurs in that sense. The idea of deity as such, had not yet been formed. Even the old Hindu commentators say that what they mean by devatd, is simply whatever or who ever is addressed in a hymn, the object of the hymn, while they call rishi or seer, whoever ad dresses anything or anybody, the subject of the hymn. Thus when a victim that has to be offered is addressed, or even a sacrificial vessel, or a chariot, or a battle-axe, or a shield, all these are called devatds. In some dialogues which are found among the hymns, whoever speaks is called the rishi, who ever is spoken to is the devatd. Devatd has become in fact a technical term, and means no more in the language of native theologians than the object ad dressed by the poet. But though the abstract term devatd, deity, does not yet occur in the hymns of the Rig- Veda, we find that most of the beings to whom the ancient poets of India addressed their hymns, were called deva. If the Greeks had to translate this deva into Greek, they would probably use Oeos, just as we translate the Greek Oeoi by gods, without much thinking what we mean by that term. But when we ask ourselves what thoughts the Vedic poets connected with the word deva, we shall find that they were very different from the thoughts expressed by the Greek Oeos or the English god ; and that even in the Veda, the Brahnia^as, the Ara?<yakas and Sutras, the meaning of that word is constantly grow ing and changing. The true meaning of deva is its history, beginning from its etymology and ending with its latest definition.

Deva, from the root div, to shine, meant originally OBJECTS TANGIBLE, SEMI-TANGIBLE, INTANGIBLE. 197 bright : the dictionaries give its meaning as god or divine. But if in translating the hymns of the Veda we always translate deva by deus, or by god, we should sometimes commit a mental anachronism of a thousand years. At the time of which we are nqw speaking, gods, in our sense of the word, did not yet exist. They were slowly struggling into existence, that is to say, the concept and name of deity was passing through the first stages of its evolution. ' In contemplation of created things men were ascending step by step to God1.' And this is the real value of the Vedic hymns. While Hesiod gives us, as it were, the past history of a theogony, we see in the Veda the theogony itself, the very birth and growth of the gods, i. e. the birth and growth of the words for god ; and we also see in later hymns — later in character, if not in time — the subsequent phases in the development of these divine conceptions.
zh: 語法性別雖然在後續的詩意神話建構過程中發揮了極大的助力，卻並非真正的驅動力。真正的驅動力，內蘊於語言與思維的本質之中。人類為自身的行動設有聲音符號，他在外在世界中發現相似的行動，並藉由同樣的聲音符號去掌握、去抓取、去理解外在世界的種種對象。起初，他絕不會因為河流被稱為「守護者」，就幻想河流擁有腿、臂與防衛的武器；也不會因為月亮分割並丈量天空，就認為月亮是個木匠。這類誤解，要到後期才會產生。此刻，我們仍遊走於遠為低階的思維層次。

在雅利安語系中，助動詞的發展尤其值得注意。這些動詞最初具有完整的獨立意義，後來逐漸褪去其具體內涵，轉而承擔純粹的語法功能。例如，梵文的 *as*（「存在」）與 *bhū*（「成為」）在吠陀文獻中仍保留其原始含義，但在後期語言中卻淪為構成複合時態的輔助工具。此現象與宗教觀唸的演變如出一轍：當某位神祇從具體的自然現象中抽象出來，其名稱便不再指涉特定的風雨雷電，而成為超越性力量的象徵。語言科學（the science of language）告訴我們，這種從具體到抽象的蛻變，正是人類思維邁向無限者（the Infinite）的必經之路。

我們想像語言若無句子便不可能存在，而句子若無**可觸知、半可觸知、不可觸知**的客體與繫詞亦不可能成立。191 這種觀點既對也錯。若我們按「句子」的本義來理解——即傳達某種意義的言說——那麼它是對的；但若我們認為句子是由數個詞、一個主詞、一個謂詞與一個繫詞構成的言說，那便是錯的。單純的祈使句就是一個句子；動詞的每一種形式都可能是一個句子。我們今日所謂的名詞，原本是一種句子，由詞根與某種所謂的後綴構成，而後綴指向某個被該詞根所陳述的事物。同樣地，當存在主詞與謂詞時，我們可以說繫詞是隱含的，但事實是：最初它並未被表達出來，也無需被表達；甚至在原始語言中，根本不可能將其表達出來。能夠說出 *vir est bonus*（此人為善），而非 *vir bonus*（善人），乃是人類語言最晚近的成就之一。
我們看到，早期的雅利安人發現，除非將事物視為主動的，否則很難談論（亦即思考）它們。當他們試圖表達某物「是」或「曾是」時，也必須克服同樣的困難。起初，他們只能透過說某物做了他們自己所做的事，來表達這個概念。而所有人類最普遍的行為，便是呼吸的動作；因此，在我們說事物「存在」的地方，他們則說事物「呼吸」。
> 「正如呼吸一般。」 （此處原文僅為「AS, to breathe.」，並無前後文，故依規則忠實譯出。）
動詞詞根 *as*（仍存活於我們的「he is」之中）是一條非常古老的詞根：它在雅利安人分裂之前，便已以其抽象意義存在。儘管如此，我們知道 *as* 在能夠意指「存在」之前，原本的意思是「呼吸」。
最簡單的派生詞是 *as*（呼吸），其梵文形式為 *as-u*（氣息）；由此可能衍生出 *asu-ra*（阿修羅）。
那些呼吸者、生存者、存在者，以及最終，對於活生生的諸神最古老的稱謂——吠陀中的阿修羅（Asura）¹。
BHU，意為「生長」。
當這個詞根 *as*（呼吸）被認為不適用於某些事物時——例如，明顯不會呼吸的樹木或其他東西——便採用了第二個詞根 *bhu*，其原意為「生長」，即希臘文的 φύω（phúō），這個詞至今仍存在於我們的英文動詞 *to be* 之中。它不僅適用於動物界，也適用於植物界，適用於一切生長之物；而大地本身也被稱為 *Bhūs*（生長者）。
**VAS**，意為「居住」。

最後，當需要一個更廣泛的概念時，便採用了詞根 *vas*，其原意為「居住、停留」。我們在梵文的 *vas-tu*（房屋）、希臘文的 *ἄστυ*（城鎮）中都可見到它，而它至今仍殘留在英文的 *was* 之中。這個詞可用於指稱所有既不屬於「呼吸」概念、也不屬於「生長」概念的事物。這是邁向表達非人格或無生命存在狀態的初步嘗試。事實上，陽性、陰性與中性名詞的形成，與這三個助動詞的引入之間，存在著某種類比關係。

此梵文 *asu* 即為波斯古經之 *ahu*，在《阿維斯陀》中具有「良知」與「世界」之義（參見達梅斯特特《奧爾馬茲德與阿赫裡曼》第 47 頁）。若波斯古經之 *ahu* 亦用於「主」義，並不能因此推斷 *ahura*（如 *Ahura mazda* 中之 *ahura*）即為「主」，且係透過次級後綴 *ra* 構成。波斯古經或曾賦予 *ahu* 兩種含義，即「氣息」與「主」，正如其對 *ratu* 一詞同時賦予「秩序」與「秩序制定者」之義。然而，僅因波斯古經之 *ahu* 具有「主」義，便將梵文 *asura* 解為「主」，此舉似不可採。
193 原始表達

讓我們將這些觀察應用於早期雅利安語使用者如何可能談論太陽、月亮、天空、大地、山脈與河流的方式。當我們會說「月亮存在」、「太陽在那裡」，或「風吹了」、「下雨了」，他們只能思考並說出：「太陽呼吸（sūryo asti）」、「月亮增長（mā bhavati）」、「大地安居（bhūr vasati）」、「風或吹拂者吹動（vāyur vāti）」、「雨水降雨（indra unatti，或 vṛṣā varṣati，或 soma sunoti）」。

我們在此討論的是人類最早嘗試理解與表達自然運作的努力——這場自然之戲始終在人類眼前上演。我們使用梵文，僅僅是為了說明遠在梵文形成之前的語言歷程。理解如何決定表達，各種表達在成為傳統後又如何反過來影響理解，這種相互作用又如何必然產生古代神話——這些問題都屬於思想發展的後期階段，此刻不應讓它們耽擱我們的進程。唯有一點，無論如何強調都不為過：因為早期雅利安人必須用表達各種活動的名稱來稱呼太陽，因為太陽被稱為「照耀者」或「溫暖者」、「創造者」或「養育者」；因為他們稱月亮為「度量者」、黎明為「喚醒者」、雷電為「咆哮者」、雨水為「降雨者」、火焰為「疾馳者」——請不要因此認為他們相信這些物體是長著手腳的人類。即使他們仍說「太陽在呼吸」，也從未意指太陽是一個人，或至少是一個擁有肺和嘴來呼吸的動物。我們的穴居祖先……
我們的祖先既非愚昧，亦非詩人。當他們說「太陽或滋養者在呼吸」時，他們的意思不過是太陽活躍著、升起並運行著、像我們一樣四處移動。古老的雅利安人尚未在月亮上看見兩隻眼睛、一個鼻子和一張嘴，也未將吹拂的風想像成許多鼓著腮幫子的頑童，從天空四角噴出陣陣氣流。這一切將在日後逐漸出現，但並非在人類思想的這些早期歲月。
在我們目前所處的這個階段，我相信我們的雅利安祖先，對於我們描述為半可觸及或不可觸及的物體，非但沒有賦予其靈魂、將其人格化或擬人化，反而更為它們與自身之間的差異所震撼，遠勝於任何想像中的相似之處。

在此，容我提醒各位注意《吠陀》中保存的一項奇特佐證，足以印證此說。我們所謂的比擬，在許多《吠陀》頌歌中仍是以否定形式呈現的。與其像我們那樣說「堅如磐石」，《吠陀》詩人反倒說「堅固，非磐石¹」——也就是說，他們強調相異之處，以便讓人感受到相似之處。他們向神獻上讚美之頌，而非甘美之食²——意即，彷彿那是甘美之食。河流被描述為「咆哮著靠近，非公牛」——即如同公牛一般；而 ¹ 梨俱吠陀，I, 52, 2，*sa/ḥ parvata/ḥ na akyntaḥ*；I, 64, 7，*giraya/ḥ na svatavasaḥ*。此處 *na* 置於作為比擬的詞語之後，故其原始概念為：「他，磐石，非」——亦即他並非全然是磐石，而僅在某種程度上是磐石。 ² 此處「非甘美之食」乃比擬修辭，意謂頌歌雖非實質祭品，卻如同祭品般被獻上。
據說暴風神馬拉茨（Maruts）¹ 將崇拜者擁入懷中，「如父親，而非兒子」，即如同父親將兒子抱在懷中一般。
因此，太陽與月亮無疑被描述為四處移動，但並非作為動物；河流咆哮與搏鬥，卻並非人類；山巒不可被推倒，卻並非戰士；火焰吞噬森林，然而牠並非一頭獅子。
在翻譯吠陀經文時，我們總是以「如」（like）來表達否定詞「na」（not）；但重要的是要留意，這些詩人本身最初所感受到的相異之處，即使不比相似之處更多，也至少是同樣強烈的。

在吠陀詩歌中，我們發現大量所謂的「固定修飾語」（standing epithets），這些修飾語反覆附著於特定神祇之名，幾乎成為其專屬稱號。例如，因陀羅（Indra）常被稱為「摧城者」（Purandara）、「富者」（Maghavan）；阿耆尼（Agni）則被稱為「家家之客」（Atithi）、「祭火之馭者」（Hotar）。此類固定修飾語不僅是詩歌修辭的慣例，更揭示了早期宗教思維中神祇功能與屬性的凝結過程。當一個修飾語被反覆使用，它便逐漸脫離原初的修辭功能，轉而成為神祇本質的一部分——這正是語言如何塑造神話思維的典型例證。穆勒指出，這些固定修飾語的固化過程，恰恰體現了「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）如何使原本隱喻性的表達被誤解為字面真理，從而催生多神教（polytheism）乃至輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的複雜神話體系。
在談及這些從遠古時代便引起人們注意的各種自然對象時，詩人自然會較頻繁地使用某些特定的修飾語。這些自然對象彼此不同，但它們同樣擁有若干共同的性質；因此，它們可以被某些共同的修飾語稱呼，隨後歸入每個修飾語之下的一類，從而構成一個新的概念。這一切皆有可能——讓我們看看實際發生了什麼。
我們轉向吠陀，便會發現，根據古印度神學家的觀點，流傳下來的那些讚歌，全都是向特定的提婆（devatā）祈禱的。這個詞 devatā 對應於梨俱吠陀（Ṛg-Veda）第一卷第三十八首第一節。
（其語句者為仙人，其所稱者為提婆。藉由該語句所闡明之事體，即為提婆。）

**原文：** exactly to our word deity, but in the hymns themselves *devatā* never occurs in that sense. The idea of deity as such, had not yet been formed. Even the old Hindu commentators say that what they mean by *devatā*, is simply whatever or whoever is addressed in a hymn, the object of the hymn, while they call *ṛṣi* or seer, whoever addresses anything or anybody, the subject of the hymn. Thus when a victim that has to be offered is addressed, or even a sacrificial vessel, or a chariot, or a battle-axe, or a shield, all these are called *devatās*. In some dialogues which are found among the hymns, whoever speaks is called the *ṛṣi*, whoever is spoken to is the *devatā*. *Devatā* has become in fact a technical term, and means no more in the language of native theologians than the object addressed by the poet. But though the abstract term *devatā*, deity, does not yet occur in the hymns of the Rig-Veda, we find that most of the beings to whom the ancient poets of India addressed their hymns, were called *deva*. If the Greeks had to translate this *deva* into Greek, they would probably use θεός, just as we translate the Greek θεοί by gods, without much thinking what we mean by that term. But when we ask ourselves what thoughts the Vedic poets connected with the word *deva*, we shall find that they were very different from the thoughts expressed by the Greek θεός or the English god; and that even in the Veda, the Brāhmaṇas, the Āraṇyakas and Sūtras, the meaning of that word is constantly growing and changing. The true meaning of *deva* is its history, beginning from its etymology and ending with its latest definition. **翻譯：** ……與我們所謂的「神性」（deity）一詞完全相符，但在這些頌詩本身中，*devatā* 從未以該意義出現。神性本身的概念，當時尚未形成。甚至古印度注釋家也說，他們所謂的 *devatā*，不過是頌詩中所稱呼的無論何物或何人，即頌詩的對象；而他們稱呼任何對某物或某人發言者為 *ṛṣi*（仙人）或先知，即頌詩的主體。因此，當一頭待獻的犧牲被稱呼時，甚至一隻祭器、一輛戰車、一把戰斧或一面盾牌被稱呼時，所有這些都被稱為 *devatās*。在頌詩中發現的一些對話裡，發言者被稱為 *ṛṣi*，被發言者則被稱為 *devatā*。事實上，*Devatā* 已成為一個術語，在本土神學家的語言中，其意義不過是詩人所稱呼的對象。然而，儘管抽象術語 *devatā*（神性）尚未出現在《梨俱吠陀》的頌詩中，我們卻發現，印度古代詩人獻上頌詩的絕大多數存在，都被稱為 *deva*。倘若希臘人必須將此 *deva* 譯成希臘文，他們很可能會使用 θεός，正如我們將希臘文 θεοί 譯為「諸神」（gods），而不多加思索該詞的含義。但當我們自問，吠陀詩人與 *deva* 一詞相聯繫的是何種思想時，我們將會發現，這些思想與希臘文 θεός 或英文 god 所表達的思想截然不同；而且，即使在吠陀本集、《梵書》、《森林書》與《經書》中，該詞的意義也不斷增長與變化。*Deva* 的真正意義，便是其歷史——始於其詞源，終於其最新的定義。

提婆（Deva）源自詞根 *div*（意為「照耀」），原初意指「可觸及的、半可觸及的、不可觸及的明亮物體」：辭典將其釋義為神或神聖者。然而，若在翻譯《吠陀》頌詩時，總將 *deva* 譯為 *deus* 或「神」，我們有時會犯下長達千年的心智時代錯置。在我們此刻所論及的時代，按我們字義理解的「諸神」尚未存在。它們正緩慢掙扎著進入存在——換言之，神性的概念與名稱正經歷其演化之初階。「人藉由默想受造之物，一步步攀升至上帝面前。」而這正是《吠陀》頌詩的真正價值。當赫西俄德（Hesiod）為我們呈現某種神譜的過往歷史時，我們在《吠陀》中看見的卻是神譜本身——諸神真正的誕生與成長，亦即「神」這個詞彙的誕生與成長；我們也在後期頌詩中——若非時間上、則在性質上屬於後期——看見這些神聖概念發展的後續階段。
